text.title: Antipatterns and AI-Agent Gotchas
text.summary: Common failure modes when applying API-first development, including patterns specific to agent-generated OpenAPI specs.

antipattern.description: Writing the spec after the implementation is complete.
antipattern.reason: The spec becomes documentation, not a contract. Inconsistencies between spec and implementation are never caught because there are no contract tests. Consumers build against the spec and hit unexpected behavior from the real server.

antipattern.description: No versioning strategy planned before the first endpoint is written.
antipattern.reason: Retroactively adding /v1/ prefix requires breaking changes for all existing consumers. Plan for v1 and v2 from the start; adding /v2/ for breaking changes is expected, not a failure.

antipattern.description: Regenerating server stubs after manually modifying generated code.
antipattern.reason: Regeneration overwrites customizations. Generated stubs must be treated as read-only scaffolding; all business logic lives in separate service files that stubs delegate to.

antipattern.description: Spec and implementation live in separate PRs or repositories.
antipattern.reason: Spec diverges from implementation within days. Version the spec file in the same commit as the first implementation task — breaking the spec-code coupling is the root cause of most API contract drift.

antipattern.description: Agent generates valid OpenAPI syntax but uses nullable: true in an OAS 3.1 spec.
antipattern.reason: nullable: true is OAS 3.0 syntax. In 3.1, nullable fields require type: ['string', 'null']. Spectral flags this; un-linted agent output will produce incorrect schema validation in 3.1-aware parsers.

antipattern.description: Agent generates syntactically valid spec with $ref pointing to a non-existent component.
antipattern.reason: Prism and SDK generators crash on unresolved $refs. Agents produce valid YAML that references #/components/schemas/Foo where Foo is never defined. Always run Spectral after agent-generated specs.

antipattern.description: Frontend code written against mock server silently breaks when connecting to real backend.
antipattern.reason: Mock servers serve spec-driven stubs, not real business logic. Mocks enforce contract shape but not business rules (e.g., stock validation, price calculation). Agents writing frontend against mocks miss this gap.

antipattern.description: Agent modifies backend implementation without updating the spec.
antipattern.reason: Contract drift: Dredd/Pact tests fail on next run, not immediately. Configure CI to run contract tests and fail the PR; without this gate, spec and implementation diverge silently.

rule.statement: Flag breaking changes (added required fields, removed endpoints, changed response shapes) and stop for human approval before merging.
rule.rationale: Breaking changes require consumer notification and migration windows. Agents cannot assess the downstream impact; human approval is a hard checkpoint for any spec change that fails bump diff.
